quoteFirst: All being ready, we started off from Kassala
quoteLast: on the 17th January, intending to reach Heikota within three days. We encamped at 8 p.m., and next day were off at 9 a.m., marching until 6 p.m.—temp.
title:
Life in the Soudan
Adventures Amongst the Tribes, and Travels in Egypt, in 1881 and 1882
author: Williams, Josiah

quoteFirst: Naturally of a lazy habit, his present unwonted exertions were beginning seriously to tell upon him, and he had therefore disregarded his potentate's commands to watch all night, had set an enormous alarum clock to explode punctually at a quarter to
quoteLast: in the morning, and had then flung himself fully dressed upon his feather four-poster to refresh himself with a three hours' nap. The alarum, he surmised, would enable him to be at the telephone ready with a lie at the appointed hour.
title: The Londoners
author: Hichens, Robert, 1864-1950

quoteLast: in the morning was its time for sailing, and at six it was being unloaded in Boston. The six-hour trains brought some of it to New York in time for our dinner.
title: American Inventions and Inventors
author: Mowry, William A. (William Augustus), 1829-1917; Mowry, Arthur May, 1862-1900

quoteFirst: Art. 75. No officer shall be tried but by a general court-martial, nor by officers of an inferior rank, if it can be avoided: nor shall any proceedings or trials be carried on excepting between the hours of eight in the morning, and
quoteLast: , excepting in cases, which, in the opinion, of the officer appointing the court-martial, require immediate example.
title: A military dictionary
author: Duane, William, 1760-1835

quoteFirst: Men were therefore sent out to watch that the place should not in any way be disturbed, or the dead cow touched or moved, and I resigned myself to a pleasant sleep. I awoke about
quoteLast: ; the guns had, thanks to a good Shikaree, been washed, dried, and slightly oiled, and were all laid on the table, looking as if a month of rain would not make them miss fire. A bath, clean clothes, guns loaded, pony saddled--and once more off to try my luck.
title: The International Monthly, Volume 4, No. 1, August, 1851
author: Various

quoteLast: , calm, but rather overcast. The Marylebone Road had at last become hushed in silence. Wagons and cabs had both ceased, and save for a solitary policeman here and there the long thoroughfare, so full of traffic by day, was utterly deserted.
title: The Seven Secrets
author: Le Queux, William, 1864-1927

quoteFirst: 30 came out of the outer redoubt at Gargaresh to follow the fighting column and to work under the instructions of a surgeon-captain. The other columns left Tripoli about
quoteLast: , and at 4.15 at Gargaresh, about 5-1/2 miles from Tripoli, they formed up in a square about 350 yards in front of the redoubt under cover of a hill, waiting for orders. At 5.30 they advanced, and leaving cover of the hills, moved forward about 2-1/2 miles beyond the batteries.
title: Motor Transports in War
author: Wyatt, Horace, 1876-1954

quoteFirst: The stricken sea came over the land in clouds of spindrift, sand, and pebbles, and buried everything within fifty feet of the shore in shingle. The church was unroofed, and part of the Government House destroyed. The hurricane lasted till
quoteLast: in the morning. The Indien did not return, but sailed away with all my effects on it.
title: The World's Greatest Books — Volume 19 — Travel and Adventure
author: Mee, Arthur, 1875-1943 [Editor]; Hammerton, J. A. (John Alexander), 1871-1949 [Editor]

quoteLast: the word came along that the working party had been withdrawn, and that I was to take my men in. As before, we left a few sentry groups, to be relieved at dawn by fresh sentries, since the new line was now to be guarded by day and manned by night.
title: A "Temporary Gentleman" in France
author: Dawson, A. J. (Alec John), 1872-1952
